title: It's all what you make it
body: It's all what you make it
pros: Good culture. Great programs for new agents needing direction and training. Consistent business focus and support through coaching and mentorship is available. Tools available to run a profitable business and look like a knowledgable real estate agent until you are. You run your own business and they give you the tools to do that with a great reputation of a well-known real estate brand behind it.
cons: Giving a large portion of your commission away each transaction and large monthly office fees, if you're a newer agent can add up to quite a bit of lost salary and the ROI isn't there for everyone. Technology is clunky at times and hard to navigate, constant issues. Say there is an "amazing" family -like culture at each office, but once they've recruited you will soon just become like the rest, another number.

However, I found that their approach doesnât offer a clear, step-by-step system for taking a beginner agent to an advanced level. Much of the guidance tends to stay fairly basic. After the initial onboarding, the main advice often comes down to reaching out to family and friends for business â which isnât bad advice, but itâs also something you could hear at almost any brokerage.

title: New Realtor
pros: Overall positive working environment. There are lots of opportunities to learn and network with other Realtors in the firm. Good commission split.
cons: Very competitive industry. They take in a lot of new Realtors with minimal filtering, so it can feel like there is always a revolving door of people, and they may not be invested in helping you grow. While there are lots of learning opportunities there is not much hands on learning options unless you create them for yourself. I personally felt as though while they tried to foster a team environment the nature of the business is quite individualistic so it came off quite insincere. I had a few negative interactions with agents at the office which were partly the reasons for my departure.

title: Gaslighting and a complete lack of support
body: Gaslighting and a complete lack of support
pros: Bonus structure was pretty good
cons: - No growth, and if you ask for growth or responsibilities, you'll get told that you should just be happy you have a job - No managerial feedback whatsoever - You can get all the degrees, certs, and do a million side projects to try to prove your worth while you're at this company, and god forbid someone even says "Way to go you, that's great!" much less let you have more responsibilities. No recognition, no appreciation, no support, no encouragement, not even verbally. I actually got told I should stop going to school because I don't need it, my job (low level, testing) was good enough. But then I wasn't getting any support to get more skills at work, so I should just also stop going to school and getting more skills that way? I should just give up? Lie down and rot? - Found out that my peers were being promoted over an email and that they had all been given an improvement plan that lead to increased responsibility, a promotion or a lateral change while I wasn't even offered any opportunities to try.

title: Not Ideal for New Solo Agents.
body: Not Ideal for New Solo Agents.
pros: I believe Keller Williams can work well for new agents WHO JOIN A TEAM, as teams often provide the structure, mentorship, and training that solo agents lack. The company has made some efforts to improve training, and paid programs like BOLD are available for those seeking more development. One particularly helpful session I attended was led by M.E. Cook, where we reviewed contract forms - practical and relevant training Iâd like to see more of at KW.
cons: In my opinion for solo agents, Keller Williams offers little real support. Most training sessions I attended, often led by in-office agents or lenders, focused more on mindset than actionable advice. It often felt like a yoga retreat, with lots of talk but no real tools for lead generation or business growth. On top of that, agents are expected to pay extra for better training, despite already giving up 30% of commissions and paying a monthly fee that has risen from $100 to $125. The company needs stronger, more practical training especially for solo agents starting out.

title: Welcoming Environment with Strong Support
pros: - Consistently hosts classes to help us navigate changes in the market - The coaches are always helping agents master the art of skillfully speaking to clients - Variety of offices under one market center - The CRM database is pretty easy to navigate
cons: - The amount of royalty fees paid yearly to brokerage is extensive. Plus the monthly desk fee - You will get the occasional nudge to attend events where you have to pay a high amount to attend a weekends worth of panelists talking about their real estate experience (at the end of the day its optional)

title: Fun and Challenging
pros: Working as a Market Center Administrator (MCA) at Keller Williams was both a rewarding and challenging experience. The job was fun, especially being part of such a dynamic and fast-paced environment. The culture at Keller Williams emphasizes growth, collaboration, and entrepreneurship, which made every day feel purposeful and exciting.
cons: That said, the role of an MCA comes with a high level of responsibility and can be demanding. Managing the day-to-day operations, financials, compliance, and agent support requires strong multitasking skills and attention to detail. Interacting with many agents was often rewarding but could be challenging at times, especially when navigating different personalities and expectations.

If youâre just getting into real estate Keller Williams Realty Worldwide assists your customer/client just about anywhere they need to sell or buy a home. Results corelate to effort put in.
cons: 1099 employees with commission splits with company (standard for large operation) If unfamiliar with 1099 employment various income tax responsibilities need to be considered. Part time employment is very difficult unless youâre an established Realtor or put 2000% effort when you do work. All Realty's have computer systems making customer service easier and better, but for computer "challenged" you may need to hire or continue with resources which are available.

title: Great Company just tough to make it
body: Great Company just tough to make it
pros: One of the biggest pros of working for Keller Williams is the companyâs strong focus on training, mentorship, and personal development. Itâs especially beneficial for new agents, as KW provides access to Keller Williams University, productivity coaching, and a wide range of tools and scripts to help build a successful business. The company culture is collaborative and community-driven, with many agents willing to share knowledge and support one another. The commission structure is also attractive, offering a high earning potential with a cap model that allows agents to keep more of their income after reaching certain thresholds. Additionally, KW promotes an entrepreneurial mindset, giving agents the freedom to grow their business at their own pace.
cons: one of the main cons is that success at Keller Williams depends heavily on self-motivation and consistent effort, as agents are independent contractors with no guaranteed income. Without a strong work ethic or a solid network, it can be difficult to gain traction, especially in competitive or oversaturated markets. While the training and resources are extensive, some agents may feel overwhelmed or lost without more hands-on, day-to-day guidance. There can also be additional costs for things like marketing, lead generation tools, and office fees, which can add up quickly for those just starting out. Ultimately, Keller Williams can be a great platform, but it requires drive, discipline, and a willingness to hustle.

title: Keller Williams Review for New Realtors
body: Keller Williams Review for New Realtors
pros: As a new realtor, joining Keller Williams has been a game-changer. The company offers exceptional training programs like Ignite and access to resources that help you build a strong foundation in real estate. The collaborative culture is unmatched, with agents and leaders always willing to share knowledge and support. The technology tools provided, including the Command platform, are user-friendly and designed to streamline your business. Additionally, programs like KW Cares show the companyâs commitment to giving back and fostering a sense of community. If youâre looking for a supportive environment to grow your career, Keller Williams is a fantastic place to star
cons: The commission split structure, while competitive, may feel challenging for new agents who are just starting and not closing deals consistently. Additionally, since the model emphasizes independence, self-discipline is critical. The training is robust but may feel overwhelming at times, especially when balancing learning with actively prospecting clients. Overall, Keller Williams is a great place for self-motivated agents looking for tools and support to succeed, but itâs important to be prepared for the challenges of building momentum in the early stages.

title: Great group of like minded people
body: Great group of like minded people
pros: Loved being with KW, met many great people that I still connect with even after leaving KW
cons: They do overdo the classes and all, I felt like the company took more money from agents than needed. I have never landed a "deal" because of the KW name. Many clients didnt even know my company as they chose me for me. Yet had to pay franchise fees and royalty fees alongside the split. Every agent is brainwashed into capping and staying on a high split. Instead the average performing agent would do much better in a lower split and not capping for the year. It seems many dont understand that. But it's OK.

title: The best in the business. I regret leaving.
body: The best in the business. I regret leaving.
pros: 1) working at the biggest and best brokerage means having access to more resources, better industry insight, great networking opportunities, and the ability to be an active participant or leader within the local organization. 2) working alongside other agents that perform at a high level. 3) branding, technology and services are at the forefront of the industry and company is generally well regarded by consumers.
cons: Compared to some of the new brokerages that offer flat fees and high commission splits, the company dollar paid or commission splits may seem high or expensive, but company has an open book policy and provides many tools, resources, nice office buildings, onsite staff, etc. You get what you pay for. Like most larger organizations and workplaces, KW is not immune to office politics or cliques, but the vast majority of the agents and staff are supportive, helpful and surprisingly friendly and willing to share knowledge for an industry as competitive as RE is.

title: Money Scheme | Don't ever work here
body: Money Scheme | Don't ever work here
pros: The charitable initiatives are great. There is a lot of charity work and good done for the community that is not publicized.
cons: This company is nothing more than a scheme to sell courses and training to new agents. You absolutely cannot make money working here for about a year or so because of how much you are constantly paying out to Keller Williams, a mentor, a coach, office fees, realtor fees (total waste of money). You will learn very elementary information from any mentor or coach and it will be extremely outdated training. The people that work here are extremely uneducated and the level of "good ole' boys" politics and clannishness is outrageous. It's an absolute clown show. You will have a plethora of gatekeepers standing in your way at all times.
